title: Variable
content: A variable is a named storage location used to hold data in a program. Its value can change during execution. Variables have data types such as integer, float, string, or boolean, which define what kind of data they can store.

title: Data Type
content: A data type defines the kind of value a variable can hold and the operations that can be performed on it. Common data types include integers, floating-point numbers, strings, booleans, arrays, and objects.

title: Function
content: A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Functions improve modularity, readability, and reusability. They can take inputs called parameters and return outputs.

title: Recursion
content: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. A recursive function must include a base case to prevent infinite recursion.

title: Loop
content: A loop is a control structure that repeats a block of code while a condition is true. Common types include for loops and while loops.

title: Conditional Statement
content: A conditional statement allows a program to execute different code blocks based on logical conditions. Examples include if, else, and switch statements.

title: Array
content: An array is a data structure that stores multiple values in a single variable. Elements are accessed using an index.

title: Linked List
content: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes. Each node contains data and a reference to the next node.

title: Stack
content: A stack is a data structure that follows the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle. Common operations include push and pop.

title: Queue
content: A queue is a data structure that follows the First In, First Out (FIFO) principle. Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front.

title: Tree
content: A tree is a hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges. It has a root node and child nodes.

title: Graph
content: A graph is a data structure made of vertices and edges. It is used to represent networks such as social connections or computer networks.

title: Algorithm
content: An algorithm is a step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or performing a computation. Efficiency is measured using time and space complexity.

title: Time Complexity
content: Time complexity measures how the execution time of an algorithm grows with input size. It is commonly expressed using Big O notation.

title: Space Complexity
content: Space complexity measures how much memory an algorithm requires relative to input size.

title: Big O Notation
content: Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm’s growth rate. Common complexities include O(1), O(n), O(log n), and O(n^2).

title: Object-Oriented Programming
content: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a paradigm based on objects that contain data and methods. Key principles include encapsulation, inheritance, abstraction, and polymorphism.

title: Class
content: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. It defines properties and methods that the objects will have.

title: Inheritance
content: Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties and methods from another class, promoting code reuse.

title: Polymorphism
content: Polymorphism allows methods to behave differently depending on the object calling them, enabling flexibility in OOP.

title: Abstraction
content: Abstraction is the process of hiding complex implementation details and exposing only the necessary features of an object or system.

title: Encapsulation
content: Encapsulation is the bundling of data and methods within a class while restricting direct access to internal details.

title: Interface
content: An interface defines a contract of methods that a class must implement, without specifying how those methods are implemented.

title: Constructor
content: A constructor is a special method used to initialize objects when they are created.

title: Destructor
content: A destructor is a method that is automatically called when an object is destroyed to release resources.

title: Exception Handling
content: Exception handling allows a program to detect and respond to runtime errors using constructs such as try, catch, and finally.

title: Debugging
content: Debugging is the process of identifying, analyzing, and fixing errors or bugs in software.

title: Compilation
content: Compilation is the process of converting source code into machine code before execution.

title: Interpretation
content: Interpretation executes source code line by line without converting it into machine code beforehand.

title: Garbage Collection
content: Garbage collection is automatic memory management that reclaims memory occupied by unused objects.

title: Thread
content: A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multithreading allows concurrent task execution.

title: Process
content: A process is an instance of a program in execution with its own memory space.

title: Concurrency
content: Concurrency refers to the ability of a system to execute multiple tasks overlapping in time.

title: Asynchronous Programming
content: Asynchronous programming allows tasks to run independently without blocking program execution.

title: API
content: An API (Application Programming Interface) defines rules and protocols for building and interacting with software applications.

title: REST
content: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications using HTTP methods.

title: JSON
content: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data format used for storing and transmitting structured data.

title: Database
content: A database is an organized collection of data stored electronically for efficient retrieval and management.

title: SQL
content: SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to manage and query relational databases.

title: Hash Table
content: A hash table is a data structure that stores key-value pairs and uses a hash function to compute index positions.

title: Binary Search
content: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an element in a sorted array. It repeatedly divides the search interval in half. Its time complexity is O(log n).

title: Linear Search
content: Linear search checks each element in a list sequentially until the target is found. Its time complexity is O(n).

title: Bubble Sort
content: Bubble sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order. Its average time complexity is O(n^2).

title: Merge Sort
content: Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that splits the array, sorts each half recursively, and merges them. Its time complexity is O(n log n).

title: Quick Sort
content: Quick sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that selects a pivot and partitions elements around it. Its average time complexity is O(n log n).

title: Depth-First Search (DFS)
content: Depth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.

title: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
content: Breadth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper.

title: Dynamic Programming
content: Dynamic programming is an optimization technique that solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results.

title: Greedy Algorithm
content: A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step with the hope of finding a global optimum.

title: Recursion vs Iteration
content: Recursion uses function calls to repeat tasks, while iteration uses loops. Recursion can be cleaner for certain problems but may consume more memory.

title: Design Pattern
content: A design pattern is a reusable solution to a common software design problem. Examples include Singleton, Factory, and Observer.

title: Singleton Pattern
content: The Singleton pattern ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it.

title: MVC Architecture
content: Model-View-Controller (MVC) separates application logic into three components: Model (data), View (UI), and Controller (logic).

title: Microservices
content: Microservices architecture structures applications as loosely coupled services that can be independently deployed and scaled.

title: Cloud Computing
content: Cloud computing provides on-demand access to computing resources such as servers and storage over the internet.

title: Cybersecurity
content: Cybersecurity involves protecting systems, networks, and data from digital attacks.

title: Encryption
content: Encryption converts readable data into coded form to prevent unauthorized access.

title: Authentication
content: Authentication verifies the identity of a user or system before granting access.

title: Authorization
content: Authorization determines what actions an authenticated user is allowed to perform.

title: Software Testing
content: Software testing evaluates software to detect defects and ensure it meets requirements. Types include unit testing and integration testing.
